title: Continuously Variable Hole Injection in Organic Light Emitting Diodes
abstract:
CHEMPHYSCHEM 2002, No. 8
¹ 2002 WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH & Co. KGaA, Weinheim
1439-4235/02/03/08 $ 20.00+.50/0
707
Continuously Variable Hole Injection
in Organic Light Emitting Diodes
Holger Frohne, David C. M¸ller, and Klaus Meerholz*[a][]
KEYWORDS:
conducting polymers ¥ electrochemistry ¥ organic light-emit-
ting diodes ¥ work function
Organic light-emitting diodes (OLEDs) represent a promising
technology for innovative products such as displays in all kinds
of portable electronic devices, including cellular telephones and
personal assistants.[1±3] The built-in potential Vbi, which is due to
the difference in the work functions w of the electrodes, has to
be reached before charge carriers are injected (flat band
condition).[4, 5] In order to minimize the operating voltage of an
that this information is profound regarding the base-to-base
coupling in DNA and its dependence on structural fluctuations,
especially at room temperature.[15]
In conclusion, we have presented two specifically functional-
ized DNA assemblies which enable us to address the important
issue of electronic energy migration in DNA by applying optical
spectroscopic methods. Our study shows that excitonic coupling
between adjacent bases leads to delocalized excitation between
identical bases, even at room temperature. As a result, we
observe a new, weakly allowed emission band which is red-
shifted with respect to the typical Ap fluorescence. Excitonic
interaction (with partial charge transfer character) between
nonidentical bases leads to exciplex emission, however, this
interaction is substantially weaker. Further investigations, which
will address the time-scale for electronic energy migration in
DNA, are currently in progress.
Experimental Section
The oligonucleotides A1 and A2 were prepared on a Expedite 8909
DNA synthesizer via standard phosphoramidite chemistry using
chemicals and controlled pore glass (CPG, 1 mol, 1000 ä; Glen
Research). The resin was cleaved and the DNA was deprotected by
treatment with concentrated NH4OH at 60C for 10 h. The oligonu-
cleotide was dried and purified by HPLC on a semipreparative RP-C18
column (300 ä; Supelco) under the following conditions: A
NH4OAc buffer (50 mM, pH 6.5); B  MeCN; gradient 0 ± 15% B
over 0 ± 45 min. The oligonucleotides were lyophilized and quanti-
fied by their absorbance at 260 nm[16] on a Cary100 (Varian)
spectrometer. Duplexes were formed by heating to 80C (10 min),
followed by slow cooling. All spectroscopic measurements were
performed in quartz glass cells (1 cm) and using Na-Pi buffer (10 mM;
pH 7). The B-DNA conformation of the duplexes A1 and A2 was
confirmed by CD spectroscopy (2.5 M duplex, 185 ± 310 nm), and the
melting temperatures were measured (2.5 M duplex, 260 nm, 10±
60C, interval 1C): Tm(A1)  36.0 0.5C; Tm(A2)  36.5 0.5C
(theoretical value for A1 and A2: 36C) The fluorescence spectra
(12.5 M duplex) were recorded on a Fluoromax-3 fluorimeter (Jobin-
Yvon) and corrected for Raman emission from the buffer solution. All
emission spectra were recorded with a bandpass of 2 nm for both
excitation and emission.
This work has been supported by the DFG (Wa-1386/7-1 and
Emmy-Noether-Program Fi-721/3-1,3-4) and by the Fonds der
Chemischen Industrie. M.R. and H.-A.W. are grateful for the
generous support of Prof. Horst Kessler, Technische Universit‰t
M¸nchen.

Kreutzer, J. M. Essigmann, Mutat. Res. 1998, 400, 99; J. H. J. Hoeijmakers,
Nature 2001, 411, 366.

1999, 38, 3629 ± 3634; F. D. Lewis in Electron Transfer in Chemistry, Vol. 3
(Ed.: V. Balzani), Wiley-VCH, Weinheim, 2001, pp. 105 ± 175.
[3] J. Eisinger, M. Gueron, R. G. Shulman, T. Yamane, Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. USA
1966, 55, 1015 ± 1020; M. Gueron, R. G. Shulman, J. Eisinger, Proc. Natl.
Acad. Sci. USA 1966, 56, 814 ± 818.
[4] T. M. Garestier, C. Helene, A. M. Michelson, Biochim. Biophys. Acta 1969,
182, 342 ± 354.
[5] J. P. Morgan, M. Daniels, Photochem. Photobiol. 1980, 31, 207 ± 213; J. P.
Ballini, P. Vigny, M. Daniels, Biophys. Chem. 1983, 18, 61 ±65.
[6] S. Georghiou, S. Zhu, R. Weidner, C.-R. Huang, G. Ge, J. Biomol. Struct. Dyn.
1990, 8, 657 ± 674.
[7] S. Georghiou, S. M. Kubala, C. C. Large, Photochem. Photobiol. 1998, 67,
526 ± 531.
[8] P. T. Henderson, E. Boone, G. B. Schuster, Helv. Chim. Acta 2002, 85, 135 ±
151.
[9] B. Bouvier, T. Gustavsson, D. Markovitsi, P. Millie¬, Chem. Phys. 2002, 275,
75 ±92.
[10] T. M. Nordlund, S. Andersson, L. Nilsson, R. Rigler, A. Gr‰slund, L. W.
Mclaughlin, Biochemistry 1989, 28, 9095 ± 9103; L. C. Sowers, G. V.
Fazakerley, R. Eritja, B. E. Kaplan, M. F. Goodman, Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci.
USA 1986, 83, 5434 ± 5438; L. W. Mclaughlin, T. Feong, F. Benseler, N. Piel,
Nucleic Acids Res. 1988, 16, 5631 ± 5644.
[11] A. Broo, J. Phys. Chem. A 1998, 102, 526 ± 531.
[12] A. S. Davydov, Theory of Molecular Excitons, McGraw-Hill, New York, NY,
1962.
[13] C. Wan, T. Fiebig, O. Schiemann, J. K. Barton, A. H. Zewail, Proc. Natl. Acad.
Sci. USA 2000, 97, 14052 ± 14055.
[14] A. L. Sobelewski, W. Domcke, C. Dedonder-Lardeux, C. Jouvet, Phys. Chem.
Chem. Phys. 2002, 4, 1093 ±1100; N. J. Kim, G. Jeong, Y. S. Kim, J. Sung, S. K.
Kim, Y. D. Park, J. Chem. Phys. 2001, 113, 10051 ± 10055; E. Nir, K.
Kleinermanns, L. Grace, M. S. D. Vries, J. Phys. Chem. A 2001, 105, 5106 ±
5110.
[15] A. A. Voityuk, K. Siriwong, N. Rosch, Phys. Chem. Chem. Phys 2001, 3,
5421 ± 5425.

CHEMPHYSCHEM 2002, No. 8
OLED it is necessary to adjust this difference to the internal band
gap of the electroluminescent (EL) polymer.
-Conjugated polymers are commonly used throughout the
OLED community for hole injection purposes. We demonstrated
earlier[6] that w of a -conjugated polymer can be adjusted
continuously by varying its electrochemical equilibrium poten-
tial Eeq (doping level). While there was only indirect, qualitative
evidence for this correlation (from the obviously decreasing
hole-injection barrier when Eeq was increased) this paper
provides quantitative evidence for the change of w of the
anodic material. On one hand we determined relative changes of
w of the doped polymer films by Kelvin probe measurements;
on the other we measured the built-in potential by operating the
OLED in reverse mode as a photovoltaic cell. Finally, we take
advantage of the variable w to optimize OLED devices with a
given cathode. The results confirm theoretical considerations
predicting optimum efficiency for equal density of charge
carriers.
In this study we used PEDOT (poly-3,4-ethylenedioxy-thio-
phene), which is widely used throughout the OLED community
as a hole-injection polymer[7, 8] atop the transparent indium tin
oxide (ITO). Rather than employing the commercial product
Baytron P (Bayer Corp.), which is an aqueous suspension of
PEDOT doped with poly(styrenesulfonate) (PSS), for our studies
we prepared PEDOT by electrochemical polymerization of the
monomer EDOT on ITO electrodes. The resulting PEDOT films
were adjusted ex situ to various equilibrium potentials Eeq in an
electrolyte solution without monomer (see the Experimental
Section for details). We point out that similar results were
obtained with the polythiophene derivative PDBT (poly-4,4-
dimethoxy-bithiophene), which we had used in our earlier work
as an adjustable anode material.[6]
Kelvin probe measurements (yielding the contact potential
difference, CPD; see the Experimental Section) were used to
determine changes of the work function w of the electro-
chemically adjusted PEDOT films.[9] The measurements were
performed in inert gas atmosphere under conditions identical to
the preparation process of solution-based OLEDs. There is a
linear correlation between the CPD and the Eeq of the PEDOT
layers with a slope of 0.8 (Figure 1a). The data point obtained for
the commercial PEDOT falls onto this line.
Obviously, the changes in Eeq overestimate changes in w
(slope 1). Generally, the ideal unity value is assumed even
though there are reports in the literature on investigations in
dilute solution reporting deviations (slope 0.8 ± 0.9).[10, 11] These
are attributed to changes in the solvation energy. In our case,
such explanation does not apply, since the solvent used for
PEDOT preparation is removed before coating the MDMO-PPV.
Instead, the deviation from unity is attributed to the fact that
counterions enter the film upon oxidation for reasons of
electroneutrality, leading to changes of the polarization energy
of the solid medium. By contrast, for pure-ion exchange
processes in the absence of redox reactions (Donnan potential),
Kutner et al. reported a slope close to unity.[12]
Since the Kelvin probe technique measures the CPD of a semi-
freestanding film, one may argue that this does not necessarily
reflect the situation inside an actual device. Several authors have
Figure 1. Dependence of the relative work function w of PEDOT on the
equilibrium potential Eeq. a) Kelvin probe measurements: The contact potential
difference (CPD) between the free-standing film and a gold grid (). Measure-
ments on commercial PEDOT is represented with . The line is a linear fit to the
data. b) Photovoltaic (PV) characterization of devices with the general structure
PEDOT(Eeq)/MDMO-PPV/cathode: Compensation voltage V0 for devices with a
calcium () or aluminum () cathode. The lines are linear fits to the data. The
shaded area is ™forbidden∫because Eeq Eox,MDMO-PPV (see text for details). The
circles mark the maximum voltage V0,max. c) Electrochemical characterization of
the emitter polymer MDMO-PPV: Cyclic voltammogram in CH2Cl2/TBAPF6 (0.1 M).
The dashed line indicates the first oxidation potential Eox,MDMO-PPV. Inset: Structure
of MDMO-PPV.
detected work function changes in real devices by electro-
absorption measurements.[4, 13] Here, we take advantage of the
fact that the built-in potential Vbi can also be determined in a
photovoltaic (PV) configuration.[14] In our heterojunction devices,
excitons generated close to the interfaces separate due to the
internal field given by the difference in work function of the two
electrodes (PEDOT and metal), giving rise to a photocurrent. If
the internal field is compensated by applying the so-called
™compensation voltage∫V0,[14, 15] the net photocurrent (differ-
ence between photo- and dark currents) vanishes. This corre-
sponds to the flat band situation of the device, therefore
Equation (1) arises.
V0  Vbi  (w,anodew,cathode)/e
(1)
For high injection barriers, V0 Vbi. For Ohmic contacts or low
injection barriers, an accumulation region of free charge carriers

phene), which is widely used throughout the OLED community
as a hole-injection polymer[7, 8] atop the transparent indium tin
oxide (ITO). Rather than employing the commercial product
Baytron P (Bayer Corp.), which is an aqueous suspension of
PEDOT doped with poly(styrenesulfonate) (PSS), for our studies
we prepared PEDOT by electrochemical polymerization of the
monomer EDOT on ITO electrodes. The resulting PEDOT films
were adjusted ex situ to various equilibrium potentials Eeq in an
electrolyte solution without monomer (see the Experimental
Section for details). We point out that similar results were
obtained with the polythiophene derivative PDBT (poly-4,4-
dimethoxy-bithiophene), which we had used in our earlier work
as an adjustable anode material.[6]
Kelvin probe measurements (yielding the contact potential
difference, CPD; see the Experimental Section) were used to
determine changes of the work function w of the electro-
chemically adjusted PEDOT films.[9] The measurements were
performed in inert gas atmosphere under conditions identical to
the preparation process of solution-based OLEDs. There is a
linear correlation between the CPD and the Eeq of the PEDOT
layers with a slope of 0.8 (Figure 1a). The data point obtained for
the commercial PEDOT falls onto this line.

(slope 1). Generally, the ideal unity value is assumed even
though there are reports in the literature on investigations in
dilute solution reporting deviations (slope 0.8 ± 0.9).[10, 11] These
are attributed to changes in the solvation energy. In our case,
such explanation does not apply, since the solvent used for
PEDOT preparation is removed before coating the MDMO-PPV.
Instead, the deviation from unity is attributed to the fact that
counterions enter the film upon oxidation for reasons of
electroneutrality, leading to changes of the polarization energy
of the solid medium. By contrast, for pure-ion exchange
processes in the absence of redox reactions (Donnan potential),
Kutner et al. reported a slope close to unity.[12]

freestanding film, one may argue that this does not necessarily

™compensation voltage∫V0,[14, 15] the net photocurrent (differ-
ence between photo- and dark currents) vanishes. This corre-

V0  Vbi  (w,anodew,cathode)/e
For high injection barriers, V0 Vbi. For Ohmic contacts or low
injection barriers, an accumulation region of free charge carriers
¹ 2002 WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH & Co. KGaA, Weinheim
1439-4235/02/03/08 $ 20.00+.50/0
is established close to the contact. The resulting band bending
of these accumulation regions reduce the internal electric field in
the device by about 0.2 eV for each contact.
OLED devices of the general structure ITO/PEDOT(Eeq)/EL poly-
mer/cathode were fabricated. For the EL polymer we used the
orange-emitting PPV-derivative MDMO-PPV (Figure 1c, inset).
Figure 1b shows the results of our PV investigations by
illuminating the devices with a 532 nm laser. Two different
cathode metals, aluminum and calcium, were used. In both
cases, a linear relationship between Eeq and V0 was obtained, the
slope of 0.8 being akin to the Kelvin probe measurements. Thus,
for a series of devices with identical cathodes, any change in V0
as a function of Eeq directly correlates with the change of w of
the PEDOTanode. The calcium devices exhibit a higher V0 due to
the lower work function of Ca compared to Al.[16]
The fact that the Kelvin probe and the PV measurements yield
an identical slope in their dependence on Eeq shows that the
changes measured on the surface of a semi-freestanding film
(Kelvin probe) translate directly to the internal energy conditions
It is noteworthy that irreproducible results were obtained in
the PV measurements whenever Eeq of the PEDOT anode
approached or even exceeded the oxidation potential Eox of
MDMO-PPV, as determined by cyclic voltammetry (Figure 1c).
We attribute this to doping (oxidation) of the MDMO-PPV by the
highly oxidized PEDOT layer.[17] Because of these redox reactions,
which seemingly lead to irregularities in the device performance
and which are also expected to lead to reduced electrolumines-
cence, the range Eeq Eox is ™forbidden∫(shaded in Figure 1b).
Please note that such irregularities are not observed in the CPD
investigations, namely under conditions excluding redox reac-
tions of the PEDOT (Figure 1a).
The previous investigations allow us to draw an approximate
picture of the barrier situation within our devices. Considering
the absorption edge of MDMO-PPV, we estimate a HOMO ± LU-
MO gap of about Egap 2.1 eV. When the equilibrium potential of
the PEDOT equals the oxidation potential of the MDMO-PPV
(that is, where the linear fit to the V0 data enters the ™forbidden
area∫in Figure 1b) an ideal Ohmic contact with vanishing barrier
is realized. For the calcium devices, V0,max 2.0 eV is extrapolated.
Thus, the barrier for electron injection B,e can be estimated to
be 0.1 eV, namely the electron current is expected to be space-
For the aluminum devices, V0,max1.32 V, thus B,e0.78 eV.
We conclude that in the aluminum devices the electron current
is injection-limited (ILC). Finally, the barrier for hole injection
increases with decreasing Eeq according to Equation (2). This
equation is only valid for Eeq Eox. The dependence of the
barriers on Eeq is summarized in Figure 2a.
After evaluation of the work function by PV, the identical
devices were then driven with forward bias, as an OLED. The
external luminescence efficiency for a constant current of 2 mA is
plotted against Eeq in Figures 2b and 2c for the two sets of
Figure 2. Characterization of OLED devices of the general structure PEDOT(Eeq)/
MDMO-PPV/cathode in dependence of the equilibrium potential Eeq of the PEDOT
layer. a) Barrier heights estimated from the CPD and PV measurements for holes
(––), for electrons in the Al device (- - - -), and for electrons in the Ca device
(± ¥ ± ¥ ). b) Efficiency of the Al devices at a current of 2 mA (, left axis); the solid
line is a guide for the eye. Operating voltage of the devices (, right axis). The
dash-dotted line is a linear fit to the data. The dashed line indicates the BIFC
voltage (see text for details). c) Efficiency of the Ca devices at a current of 2 mA (,

devices with either aluminum or calcium cathodes. We chose
constant-current conditions to keep potential heating effects
similar in all devices. In turn, the voltages to be applied to
reach the fixed current level decreased with increasing Eeq (/
± ¥ ± ¥ in Figures 2b and 2c). Blom et al. investigated the voltage
dependence of the EL efficiency on a similar dialkoxy-PPV[19]
and found that the efficiency leveled for U  3.5 V (ident-
ical device thickness). In our case, the voltages are consis-

Obviously, for both device types there is a distinct optimum
value for Eeq to yield the most efficient OLED within the
respective series. For the Al devices, a sharp optimum device
efficiency (0.06 CdA1) is observed at Eeq  0.25 V (Figure 2b).
On the other hand, in the Ca devices (Figure 2c) the exper-
imental optimum occurs at Eeq 0.35 V (1.4 CdA1).
To rationalize these results, we consider that the overall
efficiency of an OLED is determined by the spin statistics (the
probability to form singulet or triplet excitons),[20] the photo-
luminescence (PL) quantum yield (the probability that a singulet
exciton emits light), waveguide losses,[21] and finally the
recombination efficiency of charge carriers rec. Except for the
latter contribution, all others are constants for the devices
studied here. According to B‰ssler et al.,[22] to a zero-order
approximation the bimolecular rec scales with the product of
hole and electron densities. Assuming uniform carrier densities
throughout the device, rec is proportional to the product of the
respective currents, Ih and Ie.
In the range of Eeq where optimum performance was observed
in the Al devices, ILC conditions are expected for both types of
carriers. Therefore, neglecting any influence by the disparate
mobility of the carriers one would expect optimal efficiency for
identical barriers. Following our estimations of the barriers
(Figure 2a), this situation is fulfilled for Eeq 0.30 V, in reason-
able agreement with the experimental findings (0.25 V, Fig-
ure 2b). The sharpness of the peak is particularly striking but it
agrees with the Fowler ± Nordheim formalism,[23] despite the fact
that the latter has only limited applicability to organic materials.
In the Ca devices the electron current is space-charge limited,
and therefore the simple consideration about the barriers is
invalid. Therefore, in order to further rationalize our results
according to Equation (3), we argue that the electron current Ie
can be obtained by subtracting the hole current from the total
Ie,Ca(Eeq,U2mA,Ca(Eeq))  2 mA  Ih,Ca(Eeq,U2mA,Ca(Eeq))
Here, U2mA,Ca(Eeq) is the built-in field-corrected (BIFC) voltage
(U Ubi; dashed lines in Figures 2b and 2c) necessary to pass
2 mA current through the Ca devices for a certain Eeq. We point
out that this procedure is a strong simplification, since the
bipolar current does not necessarily equal the sum of electron
and hole currents whenever space-charge effects come into play
(as in, for example, SCLC conditions).[24] The hole current can be
estimated by making the following assumptions: First, for a
given Eeq and a given BIFC voltage, the hole injection conditions
should be identical in the Al and Ca devices, that is, the hole
currents should be identical in both types of devices. This
assumption neglects any influence of the presence of electrons
on the hole injection process. Second, we assume that the Al
devices are ™hole-only∫devices. This assumption is certainly
fulfilled for Eeq  0.25 V (equal injection barriers; Figure 2a).
Under these conditions Ih,Ca can be replaced by Ih,Al in Equa-
Following this procedure, we obtained the individual hole and
electron currents, Ih and Ie, as shown in Figure 2d. As expected, Ih
increases with increasing Eeq due to the continuously reduced
injection barrier. This is despite the decreasing voltage to reach
the given current level. In turn, the electron contribution
decreases accordingly. As a zero-order approximation for rec,[22]
we plot the product IhIe (Figure 2c). There is excellent agreement
with the experimental data concerning the optimum position
(0.35 V). This is rather remarkable since we crudely neglected
all problems known in ™real∫devices, such as the disparate
mobility of the carriers (h  e) or screening effects (zero-order
Note, however, that the experimental optimum peak is much
broader than the simple IhIe product. We believe that this fact is a
result of the ™real∫situation within the devices, specifically the
hole injection in the Ca devices is much stronger than
anticipated in the Al devices due to field enhancement in the
presence of electrons. Furthermore, as discussed above the
validity of Equation 2 is limited.
The optimum efficiency of the Ca devices with electrochemi-
cally deposited PEDOT is about half the value for the reference
(3 CdA1; not shown). We attribute this to the greater
thickness (100 nm compared to 20 nm), yielding higher absorp-
tion losses, and the higher surface roughness of the electro-
chemically deposited PEDOT films as determined by AFM (10 nm
compared to 2 ± 3 nm for Baytron P). We note, that the work
function of Baytron P is close to the one necessary to obtain
optimum performance in the Ca devices (compare Figures 1a
In conclusion, we have demonstrated a linear correlation
between the electrochemical equilibrium potential Eeq of a
doped -conjugated polymer and its work function w. How-
ever, unlike the common assumption throughout the literature,
the slope is below the ideal value of unity. The surface potential
determined by the Kelvin probe technique directly translates to
the internal interface as determined by open-circuit potential
with one constant contact, rendering CPD measurements a
powerful tool for screening purposes.
We demonstrated experimentally that an OLED reaches its
optimum efficiency whenever hole and electron densities are
balanced. This was possible by adjusting the hole-injection
properties of the polymeric anode to the given electron current,
which originates from the fixed barrier at the cathode. Redox-
chemical tuning of the anode provides a strong tool which
besides OLEDs will also allow optimization of other organic
devices such as organic solar cells,[17] organic transistors, and

Cyclic Voltammetry: Cyclic voltammetry was carried out at room
temperature in a three-electrode cell which contained methylene
chloride and tetrabutylammonium hexafluorophosphate (TBAPF6,
0.1 M) as supporting electrolyte. The potential scale was calibrated
using the ferrocene/ferrocenium redox couple (0.35 V versus Ag/
PEDOT Film Preparation: The PEDOT films were deposited onto ITO-
coated substrates by potentiostatic electropolymerization of the
monomer EDOT at 1.5 V in water containing Na(PSS) (Aldrich, MW
70000 gmol1) as the supporting electrolyte. We used substrates
structured with four stripes of ITO and contacted each stripe outside
the solution. The thickness of the PEDOT layer was 100 nm. After
preparation the substrates were dried at 80C in vacuum and then
transferred into a nitrogen atmosphere. The doping level of the
PEDOT films was adjusted electrochemically to different values in a
monomer-free nitromethane/NaPSS solution. Thereafter, the films
were carefully dried with a stream of nitrogen. The electrochemical
equilibrium potential Eeq was determined. The potential scale was
calibrated using the ferrocene/ferrocenium redox couple (see
Kelvin Probe Measurement: We used a Kelvin probe (Delta Phi,
Besocke GmbH). Hereby, the CPD between a vibrating (170 Hz) gold
grid (reference) and the PEDOT films is measured. A lock-in amplifier
compensates the CPD nulling the current which is produced by the
vibrating plate distance of the capacitor. Since the work function of
the gold grid (reference) is ill-defined, only relative data can be
obtained. It was confirmed that the work function of the reference
Device Preparation: MDMO-PPV was spin-cast on top of the PEDOT/
(5 mgmL1, 2200 rpm), resulting in films of about 100 nm thickness.
Since PEDOT is insoluble after film formation, the solution could not
dissolve the PEDOT layer. Finally, four stripes of the cathode metal
(40 nm Ca followed by 200 nm Al) were evaporated perpendicular to
the underlying ITO stripes (see above), yielding combinatorial
devices consisting of four different adjusted PEDOT stripes and four
OLEDs per stripe.[6] Each diode had an active area of 6.3 mm2. All
devices were characterized in nitrogen atmosphere.
Photovoltaic Measurement: The devices were illuminated from the
ITO side by a 532 nm laser beam. I± V curves were recorded during
illumination using a source measurement unit (SMU238, Keithley).
The compensation voltage V0 at which the net photocurrent is zero
was determined (flat band condition). Since V0 increases with
increasing light intensity and reaches a constant value after passing a
certain intensity, we insured that the intensity of the laser was large
enough to be in the intensity-independent regime, but without
photochemically destroying the devices.
OLED Characterization: I± V curves were measured using the same
SMU, and the EL brightness was measured with an inorganic photo
diode, which was calibrated with a Chromameter CS100 (Minolta).
